focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 13

The first light Elise caught was not from the broken upper room but from the cellar slit at road level, a blade of yellow moving across the dust whenever someone passed in front of a lamp below. The disused tollhouse near Porte d'Orleans stood just off the press of traffic with one shutter hanging loose and the old octroi plaque gone black with soot, as if the building had already been struck once and left to wait for the second blow. The road was still thick. Carts coming in from the southern villages stood wheel to wheel at the barrier, and people on foot kept sliding through the gaps as if impatience could thin stone or timber. A woman with a rabbit trussed by the hind legs had her basket hooked into the crook of one elbow and a sleeping child on the other hip. Two apprentices in flour-dusted sleeves were sharing a heel of bread. A carter sat on his shaft and scratched the neck of his horse with the tenderness of a priest touching a relic. The last of the day had gone the color of old brass.

Lucien stopped in the lee of a wall to get his breath without announcing that he needed to. Sweat had darkened the collar of his shirt. The newspaper parcel in his coat pocket had bled through; she could smell apricots before she saw the dark stain. He had carried the apricots all day and had still not thrown them away.

"It's him," Elise said.

The tollhouse ought to have been dark. Nobody used it now except boys taking pisses in bad weather and men who wanted a place where no one respectable would follow.

Lucien put one hand on the wall. His cough stayed where it lived these days, just behind the breastbone, and did not come out. "If Ferrand is with him, he won't have come to talk sense."

"He didn't come for sense in the first place."

Another sound reached the...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...to save him."

Ferrand's grin came back in pieces. "Then you'd better be quick, old man."

Lucien's hand tightened once in the front of Ferrand's shirt, bunching the cloth at the collarbone. Not enough to make a scene of it. Enough that Ferrand felt the strength still left in him. "Stay under the hill," Lucien said.

Then he let go and turned away before Ferrand could choose a response that would keep his dignity intact.

Elise followed Lucien up the passage at a run. Behind them the ration room was already changing shape around the absence: Clemence dragging Lucie back toward bed, Ferrand swearing low and venomous, the lamp hiss, the chalky scrape of someone's boot on stone. Ahead lay the wine-shop stairs and after them the city, indecently alive in the dark.

When they came out aboveground, night had not made Paris quieter. It had only changed the register. Crockery rang from a cafe. A hand-organ wheezed somewhere near the barrier. Laughter spilled from a door and was cut off when it shut. The air smelled of dust, beer, hot horse, and the last cherries turning sweet in the market baskets. Abel was out in it with gelignite warm against his leg and a pistol in his belt and nineteen years' worth of shame, vanity, hunger, and love all looking for one place to detonate.

Lucien coughed once on the pavement, bent, mastered it, and pointed toward the split of the road without speaking. Elise took the other side by instinct. Neither of them touched the other. They moved fast enough that anyone watching might have thought they were late for something ordinary, and that was the worst part. The city kept its evening, and Elise knew it would have the stalls up by morning, while somewhere inside it a boy with burned fingertips went looking for a blast he could mistake for courage.
